We reuse our prior pipeline without modification; it computes dense optical-flow features, aggregates speed and direction histograms, and classifies with a support vector machine. We apply this pipeline to imaging-sonar video recorded inside a marine fish cage. Using a day-night dataset of one-second clips, the method demonstrates robust performance under low visibility. These findings indicate that the unchanged feature design and the same SVM-based classification framework generalize across modalities from optical to acoustic video, and they support future automatic control of feeding independent of lighting and water clarity.

title: Path Planning and Guidance for Quadruple Legged Robots Through Obstacles Using YoloV8 and A-Star: Outdoor Field Test Validation
authors: Zhang Wendong, Ni Xiaoya, Li Weihua, Boo Cheong Khoo, Sutthiphong Srigrarom
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Intelligent Autonomous Systems (ICoIAS)
abstract: This paper proposes a navigation solution for quadruped robots. It leveraged YOLO V8 for obstacle recognition and integrates an improved A* algorithm for path planning. Data was collected via cameras on the robot. YOLO V8 detected obstacles in RGB images and maps them to depth images to acquire distance data. The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{A}^{*}$</tex> algorithm then planned the path based on these results. To smooth the A* - generated path, the BSpline algorithm was incorporated, ensuring the robot moved more smoothly and efficiently. The feasibility of this solution was validated through simulations in the ROS2 - IsaacSim environment.

title: A Game-Theoretic Reinforcement Learning Framework for Disruption-Aware Resource Allocation and Inventory Control in Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Jiarui Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: In modern cyber-physical systems (CPS) with increasingly complex resource allocation networks, frequent service disruptions and user hoarding behaviors bring significant challenges to intelligent decision-making systems. This paper proposes a two-phase dynamic optimization framework based on game-theoretic and reinforcement learning algorithms to address resource allocation and inventory control strategies under disruption risks and user hoarding. The first phase adopts a real-time pricing algorithm to mitigate demand volatility, while the second phase develops a hybrid pricing mechanism that adaptively adjusts according to hoarding penalty coefficients and disruption probability distributions. Moreover, the framework introduces a multi-dimensional inventory optimization algorithm considering user hoarding intensity, storage costs, and disruption likelihood, and implements a dual-period replenishment strategy combining predictive analytics and reactive adjustments. Theoretical analysis determines the bounds of system revenue under different hoarding cost scenarios, and simulation experiments verify that the proposed approach outperforms traditional static strategies by 23-37% in disrupted environments. This research offers critical insights for designing intelligent resource allocation systems in smart grids, cloud computing platforms, and other cyber-physical infrastructure affected by supply chain instabilities.

title: Performance Evaluation of Popular Visual Classifiers on Plant Leaf Classification
authors: Miguel Diogo, Luís Conde Bento, Paulo Peixoto
publish: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Engineering, Technology, and Innovation (ICE/ITMC)
abstract: Plant leaf classification involves recognizing and grouping plant species by analyzing their leaf features, including patterns, shapes, textures, and veins. Recent advances in this field have focused on improving the accuracy of plant classification by using machine learning techniques. These techniques involve training models with large datasets of plant images and using them to identify different plant species. However, these models face limitations due to their dependence on large amounts of training data, which can be difficult to obtain for many plant species. This study aims to review and analyze various popular visual classifier methodologies for image RGB(Red, Green, Blue) classification. The classifiers were trained under consistent conditions, with training resources limited to the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 ~ G B}$</tex> memory of the NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3080. The objective is to investigate whether models pre-trained on datasets like ImageNet, which include vegetation only as background information, can extract meaningful plant leaf features. The evaluation was conducted on a plant leaf dataset called “A Data Repository of Leaf Images: Practice towards Plant Conservation with Plant Pathology”. The results showed that most convolutional networks achieved encouraging accuracy ranging from 90 % to 100 %. The only network that performed poorly was the visual transformers, which had an accuracy of around 65 %. These findings suggest that models pre-trained on ImageNet perform better or are similar to models trained from scratch or fine-tuned.

title:
Compact Physics-Informed Modeling of β-Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Drain-Extended Junctionless FETs and Its Integration in Buck Converter Topology
authors: V R Manukrishna, K S Nikhil
publish: 2025 12th National Power Electronics Conference (NPEC)
abstract: This work presents the design and simulation of a buck converter employing a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex>-based Drain-Extended Junctionless Field Effect Transistor (De-JLFET), modeled to capture both channel and drift region conduction. A physically insightful two-current-source Verilog-A model is developed, where one current source represents the intrinsic channel current and the other models the drift current through the extended drain. The model is integrated into a buck converter topology, enabling accurate device-circuit co-simulation within the Cadence Virtuoso environment. Converter performance is validated by varying the gate drive duty cycles and load resistance from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \mathrm{k} \Omega$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \mathrm{k} \Omega$</tex>, demonstrating stable output regulation and highlighting the efficiency potential of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> De-JLFETs for high-voltage power conversion applications. The results reinforce the suitability of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta-\text{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex> as a wide bandgap material for future low-loss, compact power electronics.

title: Economic Dispatch of Microgrid Clusters Based on Triangulation Topology Aggregation Optimizer Considering Electric Vehicles
authors: Chengye Sui
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: The increasing adoption of renewable energy and microgrids necessitates advanced economic dispatch for interconnected multi-microgrid (MMG) systems. This paper applies a novel meta-heuristic algorithm, the triangulation topology aggregation optimizer (TTAO), to solve the MMG economic dispatch problem. The TTAO is inspired by geometric similarity principles, utilizing triangular topological units and dual strategies—Generic Aggregation and Local Aggregation—to effectively balance global exploration and local exploitation. Simulation results on a three-microgrid cluster demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves the lowest total daily operating cost (approximately 7,000 CNY) and exhibits superior convergence performance compared to benchmark algorithms such as GWO, PSO, and GA, validating its effectiveness and efficiency in MMG energy management.

title: BCR-Net: A Scale-Adaptive Business Card Recognition Network with Coordinate Attention
authors: Xiaojin Chen, Wenshan Xu, Xiaoyang Kuang, Changjun Yu, Fen Yang, Jianrong Yang
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: Automated Business Card Recognition (BCR) is significant to office automation. However, challenges such as complex layouts, background interference, and significant text scale variance have hindered existing methods from effectively balancing feature extraction and multi-scale fusion. To address these issues, this paper proposes BCR-Net, a robust recognition network leveraging Coordinate Attention (CA) and Adaptive Scale Fusion (ASF). First, considering the high aspect ratios and discrete distribution of business card text, the CA mechanism is incorporated into the backbone. By aggregating bidirectional spatial information, it suppresses background noise while enhancing the model's position awareness and long-range dependency capturing capabilities for elongated text lines. Second, the ASF module is designed to optimize the traditional Feature Pyramid Network (FPN). By utilizing a gating mechanism to dynamically learn fusion weights based on semantic context, it achieves adaptive weighting of deep semantic and shallow detailed features, significantly improving the representation robustness for micro-scale text. Experimental results demonstrate that BCR-Net achieves an F-measure of 88.0 % on a self-collected dataset, a 5.8 % improvement over the baseline. While maintaining high inference efficiency, the proposed method effectively resolves the bottleneck of multi-scale recognition under complex backgrounds, demonstrating superior robustness and practicality.

title: AI-driven Adaptive Grid Operation Status Anomaly Diagnosis Model for New Power Systems
authors: Zuyuan Huang, Xiaoping Peng, Mei Zhang, Min Xu, Yanjie Zhu
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Clean Energy Storage and Power Engineering (CESPE)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is developing rapidly. Due to its advantages such as powerful data processing capabilities and autonomous learning abilities, it has been widely applied in the abnormal diagnosis scenarios of power systems. The current abnormal diagnosis methods for power grid operation status have problems such as topological transients, data loss, poor adaptability and low accuracy in extreme scenarios. The research designed a dynamic adaptive mechanism based on the cross-modal attention mechanism and conditional generation network (CGAN). A multi-scale spatial feature decoupling method was constructed based on Spatial-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network (ST-GCN) and multi-head attention mechanisms (MHA); A causal relationship reasoning engine is proposed based on the knowledge graph. Based on the above content, an AI-driven adaptive abnormal diagnosis model for power grid operation status was designed. The performance of the proposed model was compared and analyzed with other models. The results showed that the F1 score and accuracy of the proposed model were 97.22% and 88.34% respectively, both of which were superior to the comparison models. Subsequently, the application effect of the proposed model was analyzed in comparison with other models. The results showed that the average missed diagnosis rate and the average false alarm rate of the proposed model were 1.03% and 1.84% respectively, which were significantly better than those of the comparison models. The above results indicate that the proposed model is effective and practical. It helps to improve the accuracy and adaptability of abnormal diagnosis of power grid operation status, and can provide a theoretical basis for the related research field of power system power grid operation status diagnosis.

title: An Integrated Multimodal Machine-Learning Framework for Robust Cardiovascular Disease Prediction
authors: B. Kalaimughil, Suri Vishnu Vardhan Reddy, Thalapareddy Narasimha Reddy, Vakacharla Guru Lahari, Revanthkumar K
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is a major global health concern, necessitating accurate, early, and reliable prediction systems. This study aims to design a unified multimodal machine-learning framework that integrates clinical, laboratory, ECG, and imaging features for enhanced CVD prediction. The objective is to overcome the limitations of traditional methods that rely on limited predictors, exhibit poor calibration, and fail to generalize to diverse patient groups. Existing single-modality models, such as logistic regression–based risk scores (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \mathbf{0. 7 2}$</tex>) and standalone ECG-based classifiers (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.78$</tex>), struggle to capture complex cardiovascular patterns and often misclassify borderline-risk patients. The proposed multimodal framework employs gradient-boosted trees for tabular data, CNN-based ECG and imaging encoders, and a fusion network for joint representation learning. Advantages include improved robustness, richer physiological understanding, superior handling of missing data, and clinically interpretable explanations using SHAP and Grad-CAM. Simulated experimental results show significant performance improvement, with the proposed model achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.91, accuracy of 88.6%, sensitivity of 90.2%, specificity of 86.7%, and Brier score of 0.11, outperforming all baselines. The findings demonstrate that multimodal fusion provides more reliable and actionable CVD risk prediction than existing approaches.

title: Ultra-Compact and Low-Loss 1×9 Optical Power Splitter Using Cascaded 1×3 MMI Structures for PICs
authors: Ravi Roushan Kumar, Prantik Basu, Devendra Chack
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Communication and Computers (ELEXCOM)
abstract: This paper offers a 3dB optical power splitter based on silicon-on-insulator (SOI) technology that gives nine equal outputs (1×9) at C-band, nearly the wavelength of 1550 nm. The architecture employs cascaded 3×3 multimode interference (MMI) couplers on a 220 nm silicon core situated above a 2–3 μm buried oxide layer. The MMI configuration is fine-tuned using 3D finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations. Initially, one input is divided into three via a 3 × 3 MMI, and then each of those three channels is further split into three additional channels using identical MMIs, resulting in a total of nine outputs. Each output has approximately 1/9th of the input. with minimal excess loss, 0.1 dB for 3 × 3 and 0.2 dB for 1 × 9.

title: Optimizing Homomorphic Encryption in Federated Learning with Zero-Skipping
authors: Yoo-Bin Tae, Soo-Jeong Park, Geon-Ha Kim, Seung-Ho Lim
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Federated learning enables training AI model without sharing of data, but requires protections such as homomorphic encryption to prevent reverse engineering of shared gradients, which incurs significant computational overhead. To mitigate this overhead, we propose an improved compression scheme for homomorphic encryption that integrates two key optimizations: clipped quantization and zero-skipping encryption. Clipped quantization increases the proportion of zeros during gradient quantization, while zero-skipping encryption identifies blocks that are quantized to zero and omits homomorphic encryption, thereby reducing the overhead of homomorphic encryption. The preliminary experiments for the federated learning with CNN show that the improved homomorphic encryption method could reduce client-side homomorphic encryption time and overall training time with a small decrease in accuracy.

title: Frequency-Dependent Magnetization Behaviour of Solid-Steel Core Materials for Axial Electromagnetic Bearings
authors: Aditya Raj, Shahjahan Ahmad Syed, G. Narayanan
publish: 2025 12th National Power Electronics Conference (NPEC)
abstract: Axial electromagnetic bearings are being increasingly used to support high-speed rotors coupled to compressors and turbines, against axial loads. These are made of solid high-conductivity steels, such as EN8, which can support more than one tesla of magnetic field. Due to this, significant eddy currents get induced in the core even at tens of hertz of flux reversal frequency. These eddy currents in core adversely affect its magnetization behaviour, depending upon its material and geometry. This paper considers a solid EN8 steel in form of three toroidal shaped cores of identical outer and inner diameters but with different axial thicknesses. The core samples are excited with sinusoidal flux-densities over a frequency range of 0.5 Hz to 500 Hz, spanning three decades. Frequency dependence of measured flux-linkage versus current characteristics is categorised into three distinct frequency ranges. It is shown that the low frequency characteristics are fairly independent of frequency and axial thickness, and mainly depend upon the B-H relation of core material. In the intermediate frequency range, the flux-linkage versus current loops get broader, indicating an increased core loss, but without affecting the peak flux-linkages obtained. In the high-frequency range, peak flux-linkages for a given peak coil current reduce significantly. This may lead to reduced force producing capability at higher frequencies, when such material is used in cores of axial electromagnetic bearings. Finite Element based studies are presented to better understand the experimental findings. Eddy currents in the core and the consequent nonuniformity in the flux-density distribution inside the core are found to be the major reasons for the frequency dependent magnetization behaviour.

title: Secure Yet Practical PHR Sharing: A Hybrid Approach of NFT and Attribute-Based Encryption
authors: Yoshinobu Shijo, Nanami Miyanishi, Shogo Ochiai, Eiichiro Hashiba, Miki Tani, Masayuki Murata, Kenji Yamada
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Personal Health Records (PHRs) enable personalized and continuous healthcare services, but contain highly sensitive information, requiring strong security and privacy safeguards. Self-sovereign architectures, where individuals retain full control over their data, represent a promising model for secure PHR sharing. In our prior work, we implemented a blockchain-based system using Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs) to represent data ownership and usage rights. While NFTs provide tamper resistance, NFT-only access control is vulnerable to wallet compromise and requires explicit user consent, making it unsuitable for emergency access when patients are unconscious or otherwise unable to consent. To address these limitations, we newly propose a hybrid PHR-sharing framework combining NFTs with Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE). Our new approach enforces cryptographic access policies beyond NFT possession and enables emergency access to predefined medical information without explicit user consent. We analyze representative attack scenarios and show that the scheme provides secure access control and rights management. We implement a prototype and evaluate its performance. For 1 MB of data, used as a practical upper bound for text-based PHR records based on wearable-device measurements, retrieval takes approximately 1 second, while registration and access granting take approximately 12 and 6 seconds on the Base testnet, a high-speed Ethereum-compatible test network. These results demonstrate practical feasibility, with further optimization possible through faster blockchain networks or reduced blockchain transactions.

title: Autonomous Discovery of Physical Laws via Adversarial Surrogates: Rediscovering Kepler's Laws from Observational Data
authors: Ron Teichner, Ron Meir, Naama Brenner
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Intelligent Autonomous Systems (ICoIAS)
abstract: Autonomous systems must not only perceive their environment but also reason about the physical and behavioral principles that govern it. For instance, an autonomous vehicle should infer that a stationary car on an incline might roll backward, that wet roads reduce traction, or that a child chasing a ball may run into the street-insights grounded in fundamental laws of physics and behavior. In this work, we investigate the potential for autonomous scientific reasoning. We apply the Identifying Regulation with Adversarial Surrogates (IRAS) algorithm and apply it to orbital data from NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory Horizons system. Our results demonstrate that IRAS can autonomously rediscover Kepler's first and third laws of planetary motion from raw observational data. IRAS employs adversarial training to distinguish physically meaningful models from spurious correlations, enabling the extraction of interpretable relationships. The algorithm independently derives Kepler's laws from Tycho Brahe-like raw data, replicating the historical process by which these principles were originally formulated. Rather than producing analytical expressions of these laws, IRAS optimizes neural networks that accurately capture the relevant physical relationships. Understanding these laws in symbolic form remains a separate task, requiring interpretation of the learned functions. We also discuss structural limitations of IRAS that currently prevent it from discovering Kepler's second law. Recently it was shown that LLMs lack the inductive biases necessary to autonomously infer underlying conservation laws-highlighting a fundamental gap between statistical prediction and mechanistic understanding. In contrast, our findings highlight the promise of integrating dedicated machine-learning algorithms into autonomous systems, paving the way for agents that can learn, explain, and act upon the physical and behavioral structure of their environment. Code reproducing the results is available at https://github.com/RonTeichner/IRAS/.

title: Language-Based AI Tutors for Supporting Bilingual Education in Early Childhood Classrooms
authors: Elyor Matchanov, Zilola Sattorova, Anvar Utkirov, Jaloliddin Qosimov, Aybek Kalandarov, Khuriyat Khudayberdiyeva
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: AI tutors that are language based can be very promising in terms of supporting bilingual teaching in the early childhood classes in terms of maximizing language exposure and interactive learning. They may also act as smart friends to promote cognitive growth and improve the ability of children to communicate in the native and secondary language. Nevertheless, current approaches have been associated with issues of low flexibility to different language situations, lack of individualization when it comes to the delivery of dialogues, and low power to deal with code switching tendencies typical of bilingual students. Such restrictions are preventing the successful use of AI tools in early education. To address such problems, this paper lays out a Meta-Learning Based Dialogue Framework (ML-DF) which would dynamically adjust to both the linguistic background and the learning pace of learners. The framework uses meta-learning to maximize tutor responses in different bilingual situations without compromising the interest and natural conversations. The given approach may be implemented in the interactive classroom setting, where AI tutors will deliver real-time, personalized feedback in both languages, support vocabulary learning, and approach the comprehension of cross-linguistic knowledge. These systems also can be used as auxiliary tools to add bilingual teaching provided by teachers. The results indicate that ML-DF leads to higher flexibility, more individualized learning, and much more effective engagement and language retention among the bilingual learners than the traditional AI tutoring models.

title: DISC: Dual-level Incremental Knowledge Distillation for Domain and Class Incremental Learning
authors: Seongsu Kim, Minju Baek, Joonki Paik
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Class-incremental learning becomes more challenging when the input distribution shifts across both domains and classes, a scenario we define as Domain-Class Incremental Learning (DCIL). Existing exemplar-free approaches often struggle with severe representation drift and catastrophic forgetting due to the absence of replay data. To address these challenges, we introduce DISC, a novel dual-level incremental knowledge distillation framework. DISC jointly constrains the model in the decision space through logit-level distillation, which maintains predictive consistency for previously learned classes, and in the representation space through feature-level distillation, which stabilizes the backbone by aligning student and teacher embeddings. This complementary design effectively alleviates forgetting while enhancing adaptability to new domains and classes. Extensive experiments on the Office-Home and CDDB benchmarks demonstrate that DISC consistently surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior accuracy and improved backward transfer under the exemplar-free DCIL setting.

title: Solar PV Based Air Cooler with MPPT and Speed Control
authors: Jeet Dutta, Hari Nath Pathak, Bastab Borah, Himanshu Rabha, Ranjay Das, Prakash Kumar Nayak
publish: 2025 12th National Power Electronics Conference (NPEC)
abstract: A solar photovoltaic (PV) based cooling system (SPVCS) with speed control is designed and tested for circulating moist air in a closed room. The proposed air cooler model consists of a maximum power point tracking (MPPT) controller, a DC-DC buck converter, a DC motor drive fan and a DC water pump. The MPPT controller is designed using the Hill Climb <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$[\text{HC}]$</tex> algorithm. A proportional-integral (PI) controller is selected to control the speed of the fan by changing the duty cycle of the buck converter. The PI controller is designed and implemented in LabVIEW using the NI myRIO1900 module. The performance of the proposed model is tested using a 110-watt solar PV module. The hardware results demonstrate the robustness of the proposed air cooler to reference voltage changes. The overall system is simple and costeffective.

title: Investigations and Challenges of Generative AI for Agricultural Digital Twins
authors: Astha Kumari, Kaustubh Kumar Shukla, Charu Dubey, Aditya Chauhan, Anushka Shrivastava, Apoorv Srivastav
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: In this present scenario agriculture faces immense challenges like shifting climates, fading soil quality, fewer raw materials, alongside rising hunger worldwide. So, folks are turning more to high-tech fixes lately, stuff like smart software and virtual copies of real farms may refer as digital twins. Here an idea of a digital twin has been represented which can be implemented as an online version of actual farmland, helping growers test ideas, spot trends, and guess future crop behavior before planting even starts. Toss generative AI into this setup, and suddenly you've got something stronger - it builds lifelike simulations, patches blank spots in records, plus sharpens choices around sowing or harvesting times. Pairing both systems lets farmers keep constant tabs on fields, catch sickness in plants way earlier, while estimating yields using live stats or imagined changes down the road. In this study, we start off simple literature survey and then shift into how core methods work alongside real-world uses and big hurdles when using these tools, especially around information handling, expenses, or keeping things private. Through this entire analysis, it becomes clear that Generative AI boosts trustworthiness while cutting waste in farm tech, opening doors so tomorrow's agriculture runs sharper, cleaner, and gets better results.

title: Edge-Optimized Isolation Forest for Real-Time Environmental Anomaly Detection in Sensor Networks
authors: Sunitha Nandhini A, Manimegalai R, Allan Gorfor Ananth A, Antric K Nayakam
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: Edge computing has emerged as a vital paradigm in the areas of real-time environment monitoring, particularly in areas where low-latency and effective resource usage are key. This paper presents an Edge optimized Isolation-Forest model for unsupervised anomaly detection using multi-sensor environmental data. The model is trained on four normalized input features namely humidity, temperature, gas concentration and pressure representing the environmental context. By limiting the ensemble to around 80 decision trees, the proposed architecture achieves a significant reduction in computational and memory overhead which supports the deployment of the model in low power IoT edge devices like Raspberry Pi. Experimental results demonstrate a low false positive rate of 2% while achieving inference latencies under 10 milliseconds and a model footprint below 200 KB, making it ideal for continuous on-device inference. The system is capable of identifying early-stage anomalies related to wildfires, gas leaks, structure failures and extreme weather conditions, thereby facilitating proactive disaster management. The results validate that lightweight unsupervised models, when optimized for edge constraints, can achieve cloud-grade performance in resource-constrained environments when compared to the models which does not limit the decision tree leading to more resource usage.

title: Study on the Effect of Ethanol Additives on the Performance of Pure Water based Coolants for Lithium Battery Modules: Corrosion Inhibition and Heat Transfer Performance
authors: Xinhui Nie, Song Han, Chengtao Wu, Tao Long, Chuan Xiao, Xiaohui Guan, Liu Yang
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Clean Energy Storage and Power Engineering (CESPE)
abstract: Ethanol has the advantages of strong volatility and easy water intermiscibility, and thus it is often added to coolants for leakage detection. However, the effects of ethanol addition on the corrosion inhibition effect and heat transfer coefficient of pure water-based coolants for lithium battery modules are not clear. Based on this, this study firstly investigated the effect of ethanol incorporation on the corrosion inhibition performance of pure water-based coolant through glassware corrosion test. Secondly, a heat transfer test platform was designed and constructed to focus on evaluating the heat transfer performance of pure water-based coolants containing ethanol additives by calculating the heat transfer coefficients under different working conditions. The results show that the addition of trace ethanol can slow down the corrosion of $\mathbf{3 0 4}$ stainless steel in pure water-based coolant and improve the corrosion inhibition performance of pure water-based coolant, and the optimal concentration of ethanol addition in pure water-based coolant is $0.1 \%$. Electrochemical tests further showed that the addition of trace amounts of ethanol could enhance the corrosion inhibition performance of the pure water coolant. In addition, the heat transfer coefficients of pure water coolant and pure water-based coolant containing different concentrations of ethanol were almost the same, and the addition of trace ethanol did not adversely affect the heat transfer performance of pure water coolant. Therefore, from the perspective of corrosion inhibition and heat transfer, it is technically feasible to add trace amounts of ethanol (concentration $\leq 0.5 \%$) to the pure water-based coolant for lithium battery modules.

title: A Big Data–Driven Hybrid Algorithmic Framework for Intelligent Evaluation and ARMA(2,1)-Based Forecasting in New Energy Vehicle Systems
authors: Shengkai Guo, Yikerong Wang, Zhicheng Wang, Siyuan Zhang, Mingyang Luo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: A big data–driven hybrid algorithmic framework for multivariate time-series prediction and intelligent evaluation in new energy vehicle (NEV) systems is presented. The end-to-end pipeline integrates robust preprocessing—outlier detection/Winsorizing, double exponential smoothing for missing values, normalization, and correlation analysis—to ensure data quality and feature relevance. An integrated weighting scheme fuses Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and the Entropy Weight Method (EWM) via golden-ratio–based weight optimization to construct an interpretable NEV popularity index. Time-series forecasting adopts ARMA(2,1) with stationarity testing, ACF/PACF diagnostics, and AIC-guided model selection, followed by regression analytics (multiple linear regression, F-tests, Monte Carlo simulation) for statistical significance and model validation. A multi-province case study (six provinces, Mar–Dec 2023) verifies modeling validity through goodness-of-fit and significance tests, attains stable, objective weight allocation, and yields interpretable short-term forecasts aligned with observed market dynamics—indicating favorable scalability, deployability, and decision-support value for intelligent transportation and energy analytics.

title: Equivalent Modeling of Multiple Distributed Generators for Overcurrent Protection Setting
authors: Yingjie Tan, Heng Zhang, Guomin Lou
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: Reliable protection setting in distribution networks hinges on precise fault current estimation. However, the increasing integration of inverter-interfaced distributed generators (IIDGs) introduces nonlinear characteristics, as their output currents are influenced by point-of-common-coupling (PCC) voltages and specific control strategies. These nonlinearities render the short-circuit current (SCC) calculation inherently iterative, with reduced convergence performance under high IIDG penetration. To overcome this challenge, this paper presents an equivalent modeling approach that simplifies the representation of multiple IIDGs for protection-related analysis. The proposed model captures essential behaviors including low-voltage ride-through (LVRT) and current limiting fault responses. It supports DGs operating under a common control strategy but differing in parameter values and spatial distribution, under the assumption that the equivalent area remains fault-free. The effectiveness and accuracy of the model are demonstrated through MATLAB/Simulink simulations, showing its suitability for fault current computation in complex distribution systems.

title: Unintended Discharge Behaviour of a Degraded 1200 mAH Li-Ion 18650 Battery Pack: A Case Study
authors: Vishnuvarthan L, Tarun Subramanian, Ilango Karuppasamy, Murugaraj Govindaraj
publish: 2025 12th National Power Electronics Conference (NPEC)
abstract: Because of their high energy density and extended cycle life, lithium-ion batteries have emerged as the go-to option for a variety of applications, including electric vehicles, portable gadgets, and renewable energy storage. But if not handled appropriately, their extreme sensitivity to charging procedures and storage circumstances can lead to serious deterioration and safety risks. The purpose of this experiment was to investigate the practical effects of leaving a lithium-ion battery pack unattended for a long time at a high ambient temperature, then attempting to recharge it in a way that deviated from accepted low current recovery protocols. Unintentional discharge behavior in a six-cell battery pack that decreased to a critically low voltage prior to charging after almost ten months of storage was the main issue addressed. According to experimental observations, the pack showed irreversible degradation after discharge, retaining only a very tiny portion of its initial capacity and displaying rising temperatures. The findings demonstrate that extended high- temperature storage coupled with improper resurrection charging causes capacity depletion quickly and increases safety risk. These results highlight how crucial proper storage levels are.

title: An Efficient Prediction Method for Boiler Water Wall Temperature Based on Thermal Inertia Modeling and State-Space Enhancement
authors: Tianyao Xu, Zhongxuan Liu, Xinyi Zhao, Bin Li, Haoxuan Ding
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: To improve the prediction accuracy and engineering practicality of boiler water wall temperature in power plants, this paper proposes a state-space-enhanced prediction model that integrates thermodynamic mechanisms with first-order lag characteristics. Starting from the fundamental laws of heat transfer, a discrete state-space expression for the dynamic response of wall temperature is derived, theoretically elucidating the coupling mechanism between the influence of operating parameters and the thermal inertia effect of the metal wall. Based on this theory, a “minimal feature enhancement” prediction method is constructed: within a multiple linear regression framework, only the wall temperature from the previous moment is introduced as an additional feature, extending the model to a first-order autoregressive form. Validation using actual operational data from a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 4 0 ~ M W}$</tex> supercritical unit shows that the proposed state-space-enhanced model achieves a prediction root mean square error of 0.22° C and a coefficient of determination of 0.9990. Compared to a baseline model using only operating parameters, the error is reduced by approximately 70%, while computational complexity increases only marginally. By embedding simple and interpretable features, this method effectively characterizes the system's thermal inertia, providing a high-precision, easily deployable solution for boiler safety monitoring.

title: Optimal Scheduling Strategy of Wind-Thermal-Storage Multi-Energy Complementary System Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Binji Wang, Jingtao Huang, Jing Yan, Yufan Hong, Kai Wang, Jingxian Yang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: Formulating an optimal scheduling strategy of integrated wind-Thermal-storage complementary power system is of great significance for coordination operation of high-proportion renewable energy system. However, traditional rule-based methods are difficult to adapt to real-system dynamics due to renewable energy fluctuations and multi-device coupling nonlinear constraints, leading to low wind absorption, high costs, and excessive power fluctuations. Therefore, this paper proposes a dispatch strategy for a Wind-Thermal-Storage multi-energy complementary system based on Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL). First, a framework for the multi-energy complementary system is constructed, comprising wind turbines, thermal power units, and a battery energy storage system, which fully considers the spatiotemporal complementarity of different energy sources and the operational characteristics of each component. Second, an economic dispatch model aimed at minimizing the comprehensive operating cost is established. This system is based on the DDPG algorithm for solution, aiming to obtain real-time optimal decisions, thereby achieving efficient coordination and operation of wind power, thermal power and storage resources. A case study is simulated and the effectiveness of the proposed approach is demonstrated.

title: A Hierarchical Grid-Based Method for Wing Deformation Measurement
authors: Wenlong Fan
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: To address the need for high-resolution full-field measurement of aircraft wing structural deformation, this paper proposes a deformation measurement method based on LiDAR point clouds and hierarchical grid encoding. First, three-dimensional point clouds of the wing under unloaded and loaded states are acquired. Surface reconstruction is performed using Greedy Projection Triangulation to obtain a triangular mesh with explicit topological relationships. Pre-attached regular arrays of retro-reflective targets serve as feature points. These are automatically sorted and matched via Principal Component Analysis to construct a primary structural grid covering the wing surface. Subsequently, each primary grid cell is subdivided regularly into secondary cells and assigned a globally unique code, forming a hierarchical grid system. Quantification of deformation is achieved by calculating the 3D Euclidean distance between the centroids of corresponding secondary grids (sharing the same code) before and after deformation, thereby constructing a continuous deformation field over the wing surface. Concentrated load experiments on a trapezoidal wing model demonstrate that this method effectively enables global visualization and measurement of the deformation field. It offers the advantages of clear principle, strong noise resistance, and ease of engineering implementation, providing an effective technical means for wing structural health monitoring and aeroelastic analysis.

title: Simulation Study of Interfacial Pressure of 110 kV Integral Prefabricated Cable Intermediate Joint Based on Ultrasonic Nonlinear Effect
authors: Rong Xia, Junping Cao, Yuli Wang, Benhong Ouyang, Jianjun Yuan
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: With the acceleration of urbanization, high-voltage cables have gradually become the main carrier of urban power supply networks, and the interfacial pressure of indirect joints directly affects their insulation performance and operational reliability. At present, the interface pressure detection method has limitations such as being highly invasive and difficult to monitor online. Based on the ultrasonic nonlinearity effect, the finite element-based numerical investigation is conducted to analyze the interfacial pressure characteristics of the intermediate joint in a 110 kV integral prefabricated cable. Firstly, four typical detection sites are selected according to the structural characteristics of the joint, and the rough contact interface model is constructed by incorporating the measured surface roughness parameters. Secondly, the ultrasonic propagation process under different interface pressures (0 ~ 0.4 MPa) is simulated and analyzed by using the force-acoustic sequence coupling method, The fundamental and second-harmonic components of the echo signal are identified and their corresponding amplitudes are obtained, and the ultrasonic nonlinearity coefficient is calculated. The simulation results show that with the increase of interfacial pressure, the amplitude of the reflected echo decreases as a whole, and the second harmonic component appears obviously, and the amplitude change trend is consistent with the theory. The nonlinear coefficient increases with the increase of pressure in the range of 0 ~ 0.4 MPa, indicating a high sensitivity to interfacial pressure changes. In this study, the mapping relationship between nonlinear parameters and interfacial pressures is preliminarily constructed, which provides a theoretical basis and simulation reference for the non-destructive and quantitative evaluation of interfacial pressures in cable intermediate joints.

title: Research on Energy Management Strategy of All-Electric Ship Power System Based on Improved Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Wenjing Liu, Weifeng Shi, Rumeng Cao
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: Energy crisis and environmental pollution are becoming more and more serious, posing a serious threat to the sustainable development of society. It is difficult for traditional ships to achieve fundamental breakthroughs in greenhouse gas emission control, while All-Electric Ship (AES), which aims to achieve low pollution emissions, faces the challenge of low efficiency of traditional energy management and utilization. In addition, the light intensity and the ambient temperature have significant fluctuations, which affect the power supply stability of the ship's power system. The intermittency and unpredictability of the active power output curve of photovoltaic power generation further increase the complexity of energy management strategy formulation, and require the strategy to have both robustness and adaptive ability in the optimization process. In order to cope with the uncertainty caused by power fluctuations, this study proposes an energy management strategy for all-electric ship power system based on improved deep reinforcement learning optimization training. The algorithm can dynamically adjust the energy scheduling strategy according to the real-time changes of random factors such as photovoltaic intensity, so that the energy consumption of the ship is always optimal. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed algorithm in improving the reliability and economy of power supply are verified by simulation examples.

title: LLM Powered Command Line Interface Agent: A Comparative Analysis
authors: Madhisha S, Kabhinyasri S V, Aravinth Cheran K S, Pream S, Sudharsan S, Anisha C D
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: The paper presents a local offline Command Line Interface (CLI) agent powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) which understands natural language given by the user and converts it into the required Bash command. By enabling the user to interact with the agent via the terminal in natural language, the proposed system overcomes the usability gap as developer surveys convey that 69 % of the developers use the terminal frequently while only 19 % of them consider themselves as experts and 70 % find it difficult to remember the commands. This shows the need for more user friendly and easily accessible tools. To address this, the paper introduces an LLM powered CLI agent that uses Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) which retrieves relevant commands from the local database using FAISS and provides this retrieved relevant commands to the LLM which helps to generate the Bash commands more accurately. Additionally, the system incorporates a safety filter to prevent the execution of dangerous commands and executes only after getting user confirmation. A comparative analysis of several LLMs was performed and among those Mistral 7B achieved the highest accuracy of 70.7 %, followed by Code Llama 7 B with 63.7 %, Qwen 2.5 (1.5B) and Gemma 2 (2B) performed similarly with 50.7 % and 52.7 % respectively and the lowest accuracy of 41.7 % given by Llama 2 (7B). The system's ability to generate more accurate bash commands for developers is evident from these results.

title: Acupoint recommendation and knowledge graph analysis based on improved convolutional network
authors: Wenxi Cheng, Jiarui Wang, Chengpeng Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: Based on TCM, acupuncture is of great importance in treating illness as well as preventing medical problems. A significant challenge within this realm is precise acupoint recommendations tailored to specific symptoms, with consideration of the intricate inherent relationships between the symptoms and acupuncture. The conventional recommended approach is confronted with the scarcity of TCM data. In order to solve this problem, we propose an improved graph convolution network with property fusion (PEGCN). The former can extract the attribute information of acupuncture points to enrich their expression. The latter combines a GCN architecture with a focus system, which effectively captures the basic relation of symptoms with acupuncture points. And CiteSpace 6.3 is used to perform a bibliometric analysis on this point, producing a knowledge map of key words, writers, and organizations, as well as computing the betweenness centrality, the module of the cluster (Q), and the outline factor (S).

title: Predicting Remaining Driving Range of Electric Vehicles Using a Lightweight Transformer Model in a Connected Vehicle Environment
authors: Zexi He
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: As electric vehicles become more widespread, range anxiety has emerged as a critical issue hindering their development. Traditional remaining driving range (RDR) prediction methods rely solely on vehicle sensor data, neglecting the impact of dynamic environments and driving behavior. Furthermore, these models exhibit high computational complexity, making deployment on in-vehicle devices challenging. This study proposes an RDR prediction method based on a lightweight Transformer model for the vehicle-toeverything (V2X) environment. Experiments using Shenzhen new energy vehicle data demonstrate excellent prediction performance, with an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.8121, MSE of 0.1617, and MAE of 0.2961. Prediction errors cluster near zero and exhibit a normal distribution. This approach provides an effective solution for high-precision real-time RDR prediction in V2X environments, advancing the evolution of electric vehicles toward integrated traffic-IoT terminals.

title: Wearable Technology to Mitigate Bad Elevator Design
authors: Stefan Masic, Nagham Sabbour, Alexander Vicol, Iman Cheema, Eraj Zaidi, Nathes Mehanathan, Steve Mann
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: In many establishments, a large group of elevator doors are grouped together but lack visual cues as to which door will open. Persons with disabilities using wheelchairs or other mobility devices must then rush to maneuver to whichever door opens. Having no advance notice results in rushed movement that creates a safety risk. We address this problem with an eXtended Reality (XR) system to provide location indicators that show the floor level the elevators are currently at. XR technologies extend the user’s ability to eXperience reality; Elevator AI Tracking & Enhancement System (ELEV-AI-T.E/ELEVATE). aims to extend visual ability for users with mobility aids boarding elevators.Our proposed XR elevator system also provides an AI-powered assistance system integrated into the XR application, which monitors real-time elevator movement using computer vision. This system was tested at the University of Toronto’s Myhal Centre, because this building provides elevator location indicators on the first floor but none on the upper floors, making it a suitable testing environment. By embedding these predictions into an XR application, the proposed solution delivers accurate spatial guidance to users with disabilities, demonstrating how adaptive technologies can enhance accessibility and improve interaction within everyday environments.

title: Memory Safety in Linux Device Drivers: Enhancing Security with Formal Verification
authors: Janislley O. Sousa, David A. O. Ferreira, Eddie B. De Lima Filho, Lucas C. Cordeiro
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Memory safety remains one of the most critical challenges in embedded systems, particularly within the Linux kernel, where device drivers are a primary source of vulnerabilities due to their close interaction with hardware and complex execution contexts. Such behavior is even more pronounced on consumer electronics devices, where restricted resources amplify the existing vulnerabilities. Traditional testing approaches, while effective at uncovering shallow bugs, often fail to guarantee the absence of subtle or deeply nested memory-safety issues. In this paper, we propose a formal verification methodology based on Bounded Model Checking (BMC) to detect memory-safety violations in Linux device drivers. Using a methodology based on LSVerifier, we systematically analyzed newly integrated device drivers across multiple kernel versions. Our approach uncovered three vulnerabilities, including out-of-bounds writes, out-of-bounds reads, and null pointer dereferences. It demonstrates that formal methods can uncover critical flaws early in the development process, complementing dynamic tools and strengthening kernel security.

title: Cross-Domain HVAC Fault Detection Using Attention-Based Temporal Modeling
authors: Noura Alzaabi, Nour Aburaed, Mohammed Q. Alkhatib
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) systems are essential components of smart homes and connected consumer environments, where reliable automated fault detection and diagnostics (FDD) support comfort, safety, and energy efficient operation. A central challenge in deploying data driven FDD models in real devices is their ability to generalize across domains, especially when transferring from clean and well structured simulated data to noisy experimental measurements. This study examines whether attention mechanisms can improve this form of cross domain robustness. An LSTM network is used as the baseline, and a Transformer–LSTM model is formed by adding a self attention encoder to the recurrent structure. Both models are trained entirely on simulated data and are evaluated on previously unseen experimental samples from the LBNL automated FDD dataset. The findings show that although both models perform consistently during cross validation, the Transformer–LSTM achieves notably higher precision, accuracy, and more balanced confusion matrices on real data. These results indicate that attention based temporal modeling strengthens generalization under domain shift and supports the development of more reliable and consumer ready intelligent HVAC diagnostic systems. The implementation if the proposed model is available through the following repository: https://github.com/NourO93/DA Transformer LSTM.

title: Satellite Image Manipulation Detection via Spherical Space Domain Adaptation
authors: Guangfeng Liu
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: Satellite images are widely used in meteorological observation, disaster prevention and agricultural management. Meanwhile, satellite images can be tampered by image editing tools like other images. As an emerging research directions, satellite image manipulation detection aims to label a satellite image as real or tampered. In recent years, deep learning based methods such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and vision transformer(ViT) have demonstrated excellent performance in the task of image manipulation detection. However, these methods generally perform poorly in the manipulation detection of satellite images. To address this problem, this paper proposes a satellite image manipulation detection method based on spherical feature space, in which we use spherical classifier for label prediction and spherical discriminator for discriminating domain labels. Then we improve the recognition ability of the feature extractor for key information by adversarial training strategy. The proposed method is suitable for satellite images with significant distribution differences and is superior to the traditional image manipulation detection methods. The experimental results on two tampered satellite image datasets show that this method can maintain excellent performance even when using a simple feature extractor.

title: Research on Power Probability Forecasting Method for Photovoltaic Cluster Based on MIC and MQ-WaveNet
authors: Tianjiao Ma, Qing Li, Zhaoxiang Deng
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: To quantify photovoltaic (PV) power uncertainty in a region, a direct multi-step probabilistic forecasting method for PV clusters is proposed, based on key influence variables from representative field stations. First, representative stations and key explanatory variables are selected using the Maximal Information Coefficient (MIC). Second, a multi-view quantile regression WaveNet (MQ-WaveNet) is developed, integrating quantile regression with WaveNet's long-range temporal modeling capability; it employs a residual-connected encoder and a dual-MLP decoder to directly predict multiple future steps and quantiles for the PV cluster. Case studies use data from 14 adjacent PV stations in a northwest China base. Results show the method significantly improves modeling efficiency, with both deterministic and probabilistic forecasts outperforming mainstream models.

title: Research on Multi Beam Correction of Semiconductor Epitaxial Layer Thickness Measurement Based on Infrared Interference Method
authors: Fazheng Wei, Jialin Liu, Pengjie Wang, Haoran Guo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: This article establishes a complete interferometric phase analysis model to meet the measurement requirements of semiconductors. By rigorously deducing the relationship between optical path difference and phase, thickness calculation with nanometer level accuracy has been achieved. Firstly, the physical mechanism of dual beam interference was analyzed, and the specific formula for phase difference was derived based on Snell's law and optical path difference analysis. Then, the reflection and transmission coefficients of light at different interfaces were defined. On this basis, a total reflection field complex amplitude model was constructed. The final form of reflectivity was obtained through reasonable simplification and further derivation. Then, the original spectral data was analyzed and it was found that the data may be affected by instrument noise and random fluctuations. Directly solving for thickness can easily obtain local optimal solutions. Therefore, this article proposes a thickness calculation algorithm based on Fourier transform. The main frequency characteristics of the interference signal were extracted by fast Fourier transform, and the refractive index dispersion parameters were optimized using nonlinear least squares method. The final measured sample thicknesses were 7.79 μ m (10 ° incident angle) and 7.38 μ m (15 ° incident angle), with a relative difference of only 5.26%.

title: Transformer Fault Detection based on KPCA and GOA Optimization of KELM
authors: Jianing Wang, Yunfu Wang, Chen Xing
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Clean Energy Storage and Power Engineering (CESPE)
abstract: To address the challenges of extracting fault feature data from transformers and achieving high classification accuracy, this study proposes a transformer fault detection method based on Kernel Principal Component Analysis (KPCA) and the Grasshopper Optimization Algorithm (GOA) to optimize the Kernel Extreme Learning Machine (KELM). First, the transformer fault feature data is expanded from the traditional 5-dimensional features to 21 dimensions. To address the issue of high feature dimension, KPCA is employed for dimension reduction and feature selection. KELM is used for transformer fault detection. To address the issue of key parameters affecting classification accuracy in the KELM method, this paper proposes using the GOA method to optimize and determine these key parameters. After determining the optimal parameter combination, the GOA-KELM model is established. Test set samples are input into the model for fault detection. The final experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a transformer fault detection accuracy of $98.89 \%$, which is $2.22 \%$ and $3.89 \%$ higher than the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA), respectively. This demonstrates that the proposed method has strong theoretical and engineering application value for transformer fault detection.

title: A Decentralized, Offline EV Charging and Battery Swapping Platform with Real Time Station Tracking
authors: Neha Deshmukh, Vaishali Khairnar
publish: 2025 12th National Power Electronics Conference (NPEC)
abstract: The accelerating global adoption of Electric Vehicles (EVs) necessitates resilient and accessible charging infrastructure; however, current solutions are often hindered by limitations such as centralized payment systems, continuous Internet dependency, and the lack of integrated support for mobile charging units. This paper introduces a novel web-based decentralized application (dApp) designed to provide seamless EV charging and battery-swapping services. A core innovation is its robust offline transactional architecture, which enables users to initiate transactions without Internet connectivity. Transaction data, including payment and battery swap details, are securely queued in IndexedDB and automatically synchronized with the blockchain once network connectivity is restored. The platform incorporates real-time tracking of both fixed and mobile charging stations via WebSocket and the Google Maps API. The system also introduces QR-based battery authentication for secure and traceable battery swapping. This not only enhances security by verifying the battery's identity before swaps but also streamlines the user experience by facilitating quick and error-free transactions. Experimental results demonstrate high success rates for both online and offline transactions, proving the feasibility of a blockchain integrated, resilient EV infrastructure. Our work addresses critical gaps in existing solutions by enhancing operational continuity in variable network environments.

title: Synergistic Low-Carbon and Flexible Operation of Medium-Voltage AC/DC Distribution Networks: A Generalized Nash Equilibrium Approach
authors: Bowen Huang
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy, medium-voltage distribution networks face growing challenges in achieving low-carbon operation and flexible regulation. Although flexible DC interconnection devices can enhance cross-feeder power transfer and renewable energy accommodation, coordinated configuration of multiple flexible resources and conflicting objectives remains challenging. To address this issue, this paper proposes a generalized Nash equilibrium (GNE)-based optimization framework for low-carbon and flexible operation of medium-voltage AC/DC distribution networks.An operational model incorporating soft open points (SOPs) and energy storage systems is established, and low-carbon performance, flexibility, and economic efficiency are formulated as independent decision-making players under shared network constraints. By solving the resulting GNE, coordinated optimization is achieved without relying on subjective weight selection. Case studies demonstrate that, compared with conventional methods, the proposed approach enables better time-domain coordination of flexible resources, significantly reduces carbon emissions, and maintains satisfactory economic performance and engineering feasibility.

title: Dynamic Variable Selection for subQUBO Annealing Using Hamming Weight
authors: Yoshihito Saito, Koki Mita, Nozomu Togawa
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Although Ising machines can efficiently solve combinatorial optimization problems, their direct application to largescale instances is infeasible due to an upper limit on the number of input variables. An effective approach to overcome this limitation is to partition the problem into smaller subproblems, known as subQUBOs. In this paper, we propose a dynamic variable selection method for subQUBOs that considers the Hamming weight of current solutions. Our proposed method constructs a subQUBO by dynamically selecting variables that are assigned a value of ‘1’—that is, focusing on the Hamming weight—in the current solution during the annealing process. This approach is expected to facilitate more substantial energy updates during iterations, thereby increasing the probability of finding optimal or near-optimal solutions. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method through experimental evaluations.

title: Parallel Genetic Algorithms for the Traveling Salesman Problem: A Comparative and Scalable Study
authors: Divij Bhambhani, Parth Dixit, Tanuj Katyal, Jafar Ali Ibrahim. S
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) is a benchmark NP-hard problem in combinatorial optimization[14]. This paper presents a comparative study of three Genetic Algorithm (GA) variants: (i) Serial GA, (ii) Parallel-Isolated GA (independent subpopulations without migration), and (iii) Parallel-Migrating GA (island model with periodic elite exchange). Experiments on 50-, 100-, and 200-city Euclidean datasets demonstrate that the migrating island GA achieves superior scalability, faster convergence, and improved solution consistency. While the serial GA performs adequately on smaller datasets, its performance degrades significantly at scale, whereas the migrating parallel GA maintains high-quality solutions and reduced runtime. We expand the classical distance-only objective to a practical multi-objective (distance, traffic, safety, cost) weighted fitness and implement the solver with parallel islands using Python multiprocessing (code and datasets available in the project repository).

title: A Position Paper: Exploring the Self-Regulated Learning in AI-Human Collaborative Classrooms: Applications, Challenges, and Future Research Directions
authors: Zilin Wang, Di Zou, Haoran Xie
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Teacher Education (ICAITE)
abstract: The rapid integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into educational environments has given rise to AI-Human collaborative classrooms, where AI tools complement human instruction to enhance learning outcomes. This position paper explores the role of self-regulated learning (SRL) in these hybrid settings, emphasizing its potential to empower learners amid technological advancements. Drawing on prominent SRL models, such as Zimmerman's cyclical phases and Pintrich's framework, the paper examines applications of SRL in AI-enhanced education, including chatbot integration and teacher-guided instruction. It highlights benefits like personalized feedback and increased accessibility, while addressing challenges such as data privacy, algorithmic bias, and over-reliance on AI. Two primary approaches to embedding SRL are discussed: adaptive chatbots that scaffold all phases of regulation and teacher-delivered strategies that foster human-AI synergy. Finally, future directions are proposed across chatbot development, educational practices, and research, advocating for multi-functional AI tools, comprehensive teacher training, and empirical studies on SRL efficacy. This work posits that SRL is essential for realizing equitable, effective AI-Human collaboration in classrooms, paving the way for resilient, learner-centered education.

title: Risk Assessment of Matter Protocol using Markov’s Decision Process
authors: Emaan Nazeeruddin, Alireza Jolfaei, Saeed Ur Rehman, Shahabuddin Muhammad
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Matter aims to simplify smart-home integration, but its reliance on low-power wireless links and centralised control makes routine security tasks difficult to manage in practice. This paper models the scheduling of patching, key rotation, and monitoring as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) to explore how these actions should be carried out under jamming and limited resource conditions. Using the PRISM model checker, we evaluate how key freshness, attack probability, and maintenance budgets interact over time. The results show clear trade-offs between security, operational cost, and quality of service, and highlight where resource limits cause delays that increase risk. The findings provide a structured way to design adaptive defence policies for Matter-based smart-home environments.

title: From Sketch to Structure: Agentic Prompting for Business Process Diagram Understanding with Vision–Language Models
authors: Pritam Deka, Barry Devereux
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Converting real-world process diagrams into structured, executable representations remains challenging: systems must detect heterogeneous symbols, read noisy text, and reconstruct flow topology. We present an agentic, multistage pipeline that employs general-purpose vision–language models (VLMs) to parse business process diagrams (BPMN) from images without dataset-specific fine-tuning. The approach combines structured prompting with lightweight validation to enforce modeling constraints and produce executable BPMN 2.0 XML. We evaluated across two modalities, digitally rendered and hand-drawn, and compared a wide range of VLMs against domain-specific baselines. The results indicate a strong recovery of element names, types and flows and a faithful reconstruction of the overall graph structure, often approaching the performance of a fine-tuned pipeline despite no dataset-specific training. A prompt-length study quantifies accuracy-cost trade-offs and finds that moderately concise prompts provide the best balance for this task. Our findings show that agentic prompting, coupled with minimal validation, is a practical and effective strategy for turning static diagrams into executable process models with general-purpose VLMs.

title: Fusion of Supervised Feature Selection and Unsupervised Clustering for Multinuclei Classification of MER Signals in DBS for Parkinson’s Disease
authors: Xiang Lu, Xuexin Du, Peng Lun, Yande Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: In deep brain stimulation (DBS) surgery for Parkinson’s disease (PD), the accurate intraoperative identification of key nuclei—such as the subthalamic nucleus (STN)—is critical to ensuring therapeutic efficacy. However, current approaches heavily depend on expert annotations, and classification tasks are typically confined to binary distinctions between STN and non-STN regions. This limitation hampers the real-time recognition of complex and diverse neuroanatomical structures during PD-related DBS procedures. To overcome this challenge, we propose a three-class classification strategy for microelectrode recording (MER) signals to effectively distinguish the zona incerta (Zi), STN, and substantia nigra (SN). This approach integrates supervised feature selection with unsupervised clustering: discriminative features are first selected using the random forest algorithm; these features are then input into fuzzy c-means (FCM) clustering for preliminary classification; finally, samples with low-confidence scores are manually reviewed. This strategy forms an efficient and verifiable label-generation mechanism that improves classification accuracy and enhances clinical applicability. Experimental results show that the proposed ”clustering + review” labeling framework achieves an overall classification accuracy of 92.71%, with only about 10% of samples requiring manual verification—closely matching the 92.97% accuracy achieved with expert labeling and significantly improving labeling efficiency. Furthermore, the ROC-AUC values for all three nuclei (Zi, STN, and SN) exceed 0.97, confirming the model’s robust discriminative performance. By combining supervised and unsupervised techniques, the proposed multinuclei classification framework for MER signals not only ensures high accuracy while substantially reducing manual annotation costs but also offers a scalable and efficient solution for rapid neural signal labeling. This method is particularly well-suited for real-time applications such as intraoperative target localization during DBS and shows strong potential for clinical translation.

title: Fre-STformer: A Frequency-based Spatio-Temporal Transformer for UAV Human Action Recognition
authors: Tianqi Xiang, Xinghui Xia, Junsong Yuan, Zhigang Tu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Recent advances in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have expanded their applications in human action recognition. However, existing methods struggle with background interference from wide field-of-view and UAV-induced jitter. In this work, we propose the single stage Frequency-SpatioTemporal Transformer (Fre-STformer) model, which accurately recognizes human actions via mining feature variation patterns in both actions and backgrounds. Specifically, the proposed frequency based spatio-temporal (Fre-ST) module addresses two key challenges in UAV-based action recognition: spatial-frequency joint encoding suppresses background interference through spectral feature decomposition, while channel-level difference modeling stabilizes motion representations against UAV jitter via channel wise correlation learning. Finally, by incorporating the Fre-ST module, our Fre-STformer parses global spatio-temporal action features end-to-end more effectively, avoiding additional preprocessing, e.g. action region segmentation. Extensive experiments conducted on three typical low-altitude UAV datasets, i.e. UAV Human, NEC Drone and UAV-human-night. Results show that our Fre-STformer outperforms the state-of-the-arts on the first two datasets by 3.5% and 3.8% in Top-1 accuracy, respectively. In addition, on the third UAV-human-night dataset, the Top-1 accuracy boosts by at least 1.0%, validating the generalization of our Fre-STformer in low-light environments.

title: Identification of lumped parameters to model NI HTS coils using DC and AC conditions
authors: Bruno Douine, Frederic Trillaud
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: To this date, the characterization of Non-Insulated HTS coils for parameter estimation has been conducted using relatively slow current ramps. In the present work, a methodology based on AC signals is proposed to get additional parameters to refine the model of such coils and to improve on the existing lumed-parameter models for DC applications under dynamic responses. In that regard, two sets of sequential characterizations were conducted in liquid nitrogen at 77.3 K. First, a classical U(I) characterization in DC and different ramp tests were carried out to deduce the critical current ICC and the self-inductance of the NI coil. Second, AC measurements are performed at different frequencies and current magnitudes. From these last measurements, it was found that the lumped-parameter model best agreeing with experimental data is serial and not parallel as usually considered in DC. Thus, it further simplifies the equivalent electrical circuit of the NI HTS coils with a serial resistance RS connected in series with a serial inductance XS. Both electrical parameters can be directly obtained from voltage measurements and strongly depend on frequency but not the current amplitude in subcritical conditions. Ultimately, a lumped-parameter model is a useful approach to understand the behavior of NI HTS coils interacting with a more complex system or to study their response to transients in DC or DC+AC conditions.

title: Video Quality Assessment Principles Applied to Text: Spectral Metrics for LLM Evaluation
authors: Diego A. Amoedo, Edma Urtiga de Mattos, Elton Alencar, Victoria Guimaraes, Pedro V S Matias, Emanuel Oliveira, Luca Naja, Pedro Oliveira, Matheus Uchoa, Matheus Figueiredo, Cristian Maia, Rosiane Brito, Bruno Cardoso, Laura Leite, Lucas Botinelly, Celso Barbosa Carvalho, Waldir Silva, Agemilson Pimentel, Ruan Belem, Ramulo Fabricio, Alexandre Miranda
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable advances in text generation, but assessing the…
